revision.text:
[[co:commons:Category:Isonokami Shrine]][[de:Isonokami-jingū]][[en:Isonokami Shrine]][[eo:Sanktejo Isonokami]][[fr:Isonokami-jingū]][[ja:石上神宮]][[ko:이소노카미진구 신궁]][[la:Isonokami Templum]][[ru:Исоноками-дзингу]][[uk:Ісонокамі-дзінгу]][[zh:石上神宮]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Nara Prefecture, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Isonokami Shrine
石上神宮 | image = Isonokami.jpg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', a {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}} | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|34|35|53|N|135|51|09|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = {{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|lt=Futsu-no-mitama-ōkami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsu-no-mitama|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | founded_by = {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}} | established = 91 BC (legendary), more likely 2nd or 3rd century | date_destroyed = | location = 384 Furu-chō, {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri-shi|1=Tenri, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara-ken|1=Nara Prefecture}} | website = | architecture_style = [[Nagare-zukuri]] | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Isonokami Shrine'''|石上神宮|Isonokami-jingū|extra=also {{Nihongo||石上布都御魂神社|Isonokami-futsu-no-mitama-jinja}}, {{Nihongo||布留大明神|Furu-ōmyojin}} etc.}} is a {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} located in the hills of Furu in {{ill|Tenri, Nara|en|Tenri, Nara|qid=Q502196|lt=Tenri|1=Tenri, Nara}}, {{ill|Nara Prefecture|en|Nara Prefecture|qid=Q131287|lt=Nara Prefecture|1=Nara Prefecture}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1964). ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' pp. 287-299. It is one of the oldest extant Shinto shrines in Japan and has housed several significant {{ill|Artifact (archaeology)|en|Artifact (archaeology)|qid=Q220659|lt=burial goods|1=Artifact (archaeology)}}. Isonokami shrine was highly regarded in the ancient era, and frequented by many members of the {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial Family|1=Imperial House of Japan}}. It played a pivotal role in Japan's early history, especially during the 3rd to 5th centuries. The shrine is at the northern end of the ''Yamanobe no michi'', the oldest road in Japan. ==History== It is unknown which [[kami]] was initially worshipped at Isonokami shrine. Isonokami Shrine was supposedly built in the 7th year of {{ill|Emperor Sujin|en|Emperor Sujin|qid=Q333530|lt=Emperor Sujin|1=Emperor Sujin}}'s reign, or the 91 BC. However, there is little record of Sujin's existence or identity, and therefore the claim is deemed legendary. The construction of a structure that can be identified as a Shinto shrine in the Isonokami area probably dates two or three centuries later. Despite this, it is not unlikely that the Isonokami area was considered a sacred site during that time, as archeological digs have found many ritual objects, and Isonokami worship was already firmly established when they were adopted by the Yamato leaders in the 4th century. Early records show that the shrine was an emerging center of {{ill|Yamato period|en|Yamato period|qid=Q633881|lt=Yamato|1=Yamato period}} power, flavored with a military overtone. Archeological evidences shows that around the 4th century, Yamato kings and nobility constructed more and more palaces and buildings in the shrine's vicinity.Brown, Delmer ''et al.'' (1993). ''Cambridge History of Japan,'' p. 119. Prior to this, the Yamato leaders identified with kami worshipped at {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}}, but may have turned to Isonokami for its martial implications. Myths written in the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' clearly attempt to link the Yamato court to Isonokami shrine. According to the ''Nihon Shoki'', {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}}'s eldest son, {{ill|Prince Inishiki|ja|五十瓊敷入彦命|1=Prince Inishikiirihiko|2=ja|3=五十瓊敷入彦命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Inishiki|13=User:Immanuelle/Prince Inishikiirihiko|qq=}} ordered a thousand swords made and was placed in charge of Isonokami's treasures. He then founded the [[:en:Mononobe|Mononobe]] clan, who patronized the shrine for many generations. Indeed, many of the state's iron weaponry were kept in the shrine's storehouses.Brown, p. 120. When the Mononobe clan collapsed, it was reformed as the [[Mononobe clan|Isonokami clan]]. The shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|qid=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines including the Isonokami Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. From 1871 through 1946, the Isonokami Shrine was officially designated one of the {{Nihongo||官幣大社|[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 124. ==Architecture== [[File:IsonokamiGate.jpg|thumb|160px|left|Gate to the Isonokami Shrine]] Isonokami shrine is surrounded by {{ill|Cryptomeria|en|Cryptomeria|qid=Q147388|lt=Cryptomeria|1=Cryptomeria}} (''sugi)''. A ''{{ill|Waka (poetry)|en|Waka (poetry)|qid=Q737148|lt=waka|1=Waka (poetry)}}'' poem from the ''{{ill|Man'yōshū|en|Man'yōshū|qid=Q834321|lt=Man'yōshū|1=Man'yōshū}}'' anthology mentions Isonokami shrine, surrounded by holy cedar trees. The current site of Isonokami shrine may have been the site of an earlier religious structure, the [[Izumo-taisha]]. This is suspected because of a secondary shrine to Izumo-takeo found near the main shrine, but on higher ground. Since secondary shrines are never built higher than the principal shrine, some have concluded that it was an older structure that stood before the current one. The [[honden]], or main hall, is built in the ''[[nagare-zukuri]]'' style. ==Enshrined== The main enshrined dedication is to {{Nihongo|{{ill|Futsu-no-mitama|ja|布都御魂|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futsu-no-mitama|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}|[[:ja:布都御魂|布都御魂]]}}, the kami of a legendary sword (''futsu-no-mitama-no-tsurugi'') that was given to by [[Takemikazuchi]] in [[Kumano Region|Kumano]] and used by {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor Jimmu|1=Emperor Jimmu}}, the first {{ill|Emperor of Japan|en|Emperor of Japan|qid=Q208233|lt=Emperor of Japan|1=Emperor of Japan}}.''[[Kojiki]]'', ''Nakatsumaki'' (middle volume) However, the sword itself is hidden in Isonokami shrine, but the second sword is visible in [[Kashima Jingū|Kashima Shrine]], {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|en|Ibaraki Prefecture|qid=Q83273|lt=Ibaraki|1=Ibaraki Prefecture}}.The sword itself was to be returned to Kashima Shrine, but as impossible, the second sword is made in {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} ==Artifacts== [[File:Chiljido.jpg|thumb|190px|right|A replica of the seven-branched sword in {{ill|South Korea|en|South Korea|qid=Q884|lt=South Korea|1=South Korea}}.]] The ''{{ill|Seven-Branched Sword|en|Seven-Branched Sword|qid=Q912125|lt=Nanatsusaya-no-Tachi|1=Seven-Branched Sword}}'', a seven-branched sword, is housed in Isonokami shrine. It was given to the Yamato king by the crown prince of [[:en:Baekje|Baekje]], a kingdom on the {{ill|Korea|en|Korea|qid=Q18097|lt=Korea|1=Korea}}. The sword has been kept in Isonokami shrine since ancient times, perhaps as early as the 4th century when it was received from Baekje. It was rediscovered in 1873, and its gold inlaid inscription has proven to be a valuable historical resource. The sword is currently not available for public viewing. The ''[[Totsuka-no-Tsurugi]]'', a legendary sword, is also said to be kept at Isonokami shrine. According to the ''[[Kojiki]]'', the sword was used by the god [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] to slay the [[:en:Yamata-no-Orochi|Yamata-no-Orochi]], a giant serpent with eight heads and eight tails. Priests at the shrine do not allow anyone to see or inspect the sword. Ten treasures brought by [[Amenohiboko]] are thought to be housed in Isonokami shrine. According to the ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'', Amenohiboko was a prince from Korea who settled in Japan. The ten treasures he brought are known as the ''{{ill|Tokusa-no-Kandakara|ja|十種神宝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokusa-no-Kandakara|qq=}}'', and they are as follows: *Okitsu-kagami (A {{ill|Mirror|en|Mirror|qid=Q35197|lt=Mirror|1=Mirror}}) *Hetsu-kagami (A mirror) *Yatsuka-no-tsurugi (A sword) *Iku-tama (A jewel) *Makarukaheshi-tama (A jewel) *Taru-tama (A jewel) *Chikaheshi-no-tama (A jewel) *Orochi-no-hire (A type of long scarf worn by women) *Hachi-no-hire (A type of long scarf worn by women) *Kusagusa-no-mono-no-hire (A type of long scarf worn by women) ==National Treasures== [[File:IsonokamiNT.jpg|thumb|200px|right|''[[Haiden (Shinto)|Haiden]]'' of Izumo Takeo secondary shrine (a [[:en:National Treasure of Japan|national treasure]])]] ===National Treasures of Japan=== *The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'', or hall of worship, with ''[[:en:Irimoya|Irimoya]]'' style roofing, {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}} *The ''haiden'' at the Izumo Takeo secondary shrine *The ''Nanatsusaya-no-Tachi''Ponsonby-Fane, ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan,'' p. 299. ===Important Cultural Properties=== *The ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'', or main gate *Two iron shields from the [[Kofun period]] *Several pieces of {{ill|Breastplate|en|Breastplate|qid=Q858236|lt=Breastplate|1=Breastplate}} armor *Several unearthed artifacts (''[[magatama]]'', {{ill|Necklace|en|Necklace|qid=Q189299|lt=Necklace|1=Necklace}}s, ring-pommel sabers) ===Others=== *Ornate red ''{{ill|Ō-yoroi|en|Ō-yoroi|qid=Q386589|lt=Ō-yoroi|1=Ō-yoroi}}'' *Black lacquered {{ill|Kabuto|en|Kabuto|qid=Q1720878|lt=Kabuto|1=Kabuto}}, various helms *''{{ill|Kokitsunemaru|ja|小狐丸|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kokitsunemaru|qq=}}'', an ''{{ill|Ōdachi|en|Ōdachi|qid=Q384770|lt=Ōdachi|1=Ōdachi}}'' forged by {{ill|Sanjō Munechika|it|Munechika|ja|三条宗近|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sanjō Munechika|qq=}} ==Annual celebrations== *[[Japanese New Year]] (January 1) *Genji-sai (January 3) *Furufudatakiage-sai (January 15) *Tama-no-o-sai (Night before ''[[Setsubun]]'') *''[[Setsubun]]'' *[[Kinensai|Kinen-sai]] (February 19) *Kentōkōkōsha-ōmatsuri (First Sunday of each month) *Shunki-ōmatsuri (April 15) *Chōjukōsha-shunki-ōmatsuri (May 3) *Denden Festival (June 30) *[[Ōharae-shiki]] (June 30 and December 31) *Sūkei-kai-ōmatsuri (First Sunday of each month) *Boujisarae-shinji (October 1) *Furumatsuri (October 15) *Chōjukōsha-shūki-ōmatsuri (November 3) *{{ill|Chinkon-sai|ja|鎮魂祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chinkon-sai|qq=}} (November 22) *[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-no-matsuri]] (November 23) *Ohitaki-sai (December 8) *{{ill|The Emperor's Birthday|en|The Emperor's Birthday|qid=Q72318|lt=The Emperor's Birthday|1=The Emperor's Birthday}} (December 23) *Hokura-matsuri (December 31) *[[Japanese New Year|New Year's Eve]] (December 31) *{{ill|Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|ja|月次祭|1=Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsukinami-no-Matsuri|qq=}} (Every 1st and 15th day of the month) ==See also== *{{ill|Seven-Branched Sword|en|Seven-Branched Sword|qid=Q912125|lt=Seven-Branched Sword|1=Seven-Branched Sword}} *[[:en:Kusanagi no tsurugi|Kusanagi no tsurugi]] *{{ill|List of Jingū|en|List of Jingū|qid=Q120680118|lt=List of Jingū|1=List of Jingū}} *{{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|qid=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} *{{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|qid=Q3245523|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (temples)}} *{{ill|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|en|List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|qid=Q6591583|lt=List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)|1=List of National Treasures of Japan (archaeological materials)}} *[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{Reflist|2}} ==References== {{Commons Category|Isonokami Shrine}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * Brown, Delmer M. et al. (1993). ''Cambridge History of Japan'', Volume 1. New York: Cambridge University Press. {{ISBN|0-521-22352-0}}. * Kidder, Jonathan Edward (2007). ''Himiko and Japan's elusive chiefdom of Yamatai: archaeology, history, and mythology''. Honolulu: University of Hawaii Press. {{ISBN|0-8248-3035-0}}. * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|qid=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * ____________. (1964). [https://books.google.com/books?id=_6rXAAAAMAAJ&q=Visiting+famous+shrines+in+japan ''Visiting Famous Shrines in Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby-Fane Memorial Society. {{OCLC|1030156}} {{Munakata Faith}} {{Isonokami Faith}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Tenri, Nara]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Nara Prefecture]] [[Category:National Treasures of Japan]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Nara Prefecture designated tangible cultural property]] [[Category:Nagare-zukuri]] [[Category:Mononobe clan]] }} [[en:Isonokami Jingu]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[en:Isonokami Jingu]] {{wikidata link|Q708859}} [[Category:Myōjin Taisha]] [[Category:Junior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior First Rank]] [[Category:Senior Third Rank]] [[Category:Shikinai Shōsha]] [[Category:Shrine receiving Tsukinami-sai and Niiname-sai and Ainame-sai offerings]] [[Category:Kanpei-sha]] [[Category:Shikinai Ronsha]]
